title: Knuth Principles for Mathematical Writing (LLM Audit Rules)
description: Practical Knuth-style rules for mathematical prose: sentence starts, adjacent formulas, punctuation with displays, symbol introduction and definition, consistent notation, and reader guidance.

provenance.basis: User-provided distilled Knuth principles attributed to Donald E. Knuth's mathematical writing guidance.

rules.title: Do not start sentences with bare symbols
rules.rationale: Bare symbolic openings are hard to read and sound abrupt in mathematical prose.
rules.check: Flag sentences that begin with math tokens, variables, quantifiers (∀,∃), operators, or displayed math unless the symbol is part of a proper noun or numbered item.
rules.pass_criteria: The sentence begins with ordinary language that names or describes the mathematical object.
exceptions: Enumerated algorithmic pseudocode lines rather than prose sentences.
Display-equation lines that are not sentence starts in the surrounding prose.
good_examples: The function f(x) is continuous on [0,1].
For every x in X, there exists y in Y.
rules.fix_template: Rewrite the sentence so it begins with words naming the object or quantifier domain.

rules.title: Do not place two formulas adjacent without words between
rules.rationale: Adjacent formulas without connective words are difficult to parse and often ambiguous.
rules.check: Flag occurrences of consecutive inline math segments (e.g., $f(x)$ $g(x)$) or symbol clusters where a conjunction/preposition is expected.
rules.pass_criteria: Between any two formulas that refer to distinct objects, there are connector words (and, or, implies, such that, of, etc.).
exceptions: Explicit multiplication or function composition expressed in a single formula.
Tuple/vector notation inside one formula.
good_examples: Consider f(x) and g(x).
Let A and B be sets.
rules.fix_template: Insert a connective word (e.g., “and”, “of”, “with”, “such that”) between the formulas.

rules.title: Displayed equations must be grammatically integrated and punctuated
rules.rationale: Displayed equations are part of the sentence; missing punctuation breaks grammar and reader flow.
rules.check: Flag displayed equations that are followed by no punctuation when the surrounding prose implies continuation, or that use incorrect punctuation (e.g., trailing colon after verb 'is/are').
rules.pass_criteria: Displayed equations end with appropriate punctuation (comma/period/semicolon) matching the sentence structure.
exceptions: Standalone display that is not part of a sentence (rare; should be avoided).
Numbered equation displayed as a label-only line in some templates (still discouraged).
bad_examples:
We define
  x = y + 1
where y > 0
good_examples:
We define
  x = y + 1,
where y > 0.

Then
  f(x)=0,
and hence ...
rules.fix_template: Add punctuation to the displayed equation (typically a comma or period) so the paragraph reads as a single grammatical sentence.

rules.title: Define symbols before (or very near) first use
rules.rationale: Undefined notation causes misinterpretation and makes proofs unverifiable.
rules.check: Flag any symbol that appears without a prior definition in the same section or a clearly stated standing assumptions/notation block.
rules.pass_criteria: Every nonstandard symbol is defined at first appearance (or immediately before) with domain/type information if relevant.
exceptions: Truly standard constants (0, 1, π, e) and common sets (ℝ, ℕ) if the venue allows unexpanded use.
Globally defined notation in a dedicated Notation section.
bad_examples: Let δ = min(δ1, δ2). (δ1, δ2 never defined.)
good_examples: Let δ1, δ2 > 0 be given by continuity of f and g at a, and set δ = min(δ1, δ2).
Let X0 denote the set of feasible solutions defined in (2.1).
rules.fix_template: Insert a definition sentence/phrase for the symbol (and its domain) at its first use or move the first use to after the definition.

rules.title: Use consistent notation and do not reuse symbols for different meanings
rules.rationale: Symbol reuse breaks logical correctness and proof traceability.
rules.check: Flag cases where the same symbol is reintroduced with a new meaning, or where multiple symbols denote the same object without explanation.
rules.pass_criteria: Each symbol has exactly one meaning in its scope, and each object has one primary notation (with declared synonyms if needed).
exceptions: Standard dummy indices in summations/integrals with clearly local scope.
Overloaded notation explicitly declared as such (discouraged).
bad_examples: Let G be a graph. ... Let G be a group.
Let n be the sample size. ... Let n be the normal vector.
good_examples: Let G be a graph. ... Let H be a group.
Let n be the sample size. ... Let ν be the normal vector.
rules.fix_template: Rename one of the meanings (choose a new symbol) and propagate consistently through the relevant scope.

rules.title: Avoid long unconnected sequences of formulas
rules.rationale: Listing formulas without explanatory prose resembles homework and obscures the argument.
rules.check: Flag paragraphs with multiple consecutive sentences that are primarily formulas or equation transformations with no explanatory link words.
rules.pass_criteria: Formulas are tied together by prose explaining the goal and the reason for each transformation.
exceptions: A short chain of equalities/inequalities with an explicit stated reason.
Algorithmic derivations where each line is annotated by a justification.
good_examples: Starting from x=..., we substitute into the definition of y to obtain y=.... Finally, applying Lemma 2 yields z=....
rules.fix_template: Insert short prose between formulas indicating the reason (definition, substitution, lemma) and the intermediate goal.

rules.title: Prefer words over nonstandard symbolic logic in running text
rules.rationale: Symbols like ∀, ∃, ⇒ are easy to misread in prose and reduce accessibility.
rules.check: Flag ∀, ∃, ⇒, ⇔, s.t., w.r.t., iff in prose sentences outside formal logic contexts.
rules.pass_criteria: Quantifiers and implications are written in words when embedded in prose.
exceptions: Works explicitly about formal logic where symbols are the subject matter.
Short summary tables of logical forms (not prose).
good_examples: For every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that ...
We show that A implies B and B implies C.
rules.fix_template: Replace symbolic quantifiers/implications with words in prose; keep symbols inside a displayed formula if needed.

rules.title: Keep the reader oriented about goals and structure
rules.rationale: Readers need reminders of what is being proved and why steps are taken.
rules.check: Flag long proof stretches (>10 lines/paragraphs) without a stated local goal or without referencing the theorem statement.
rules.pass_criteria: Proof includes goal statements at major transitions (e.g., “It remains to show…”, “We now prove…”).
exceptions: Very short proofs where the goal is obvious from immediate context.
Highly routine calculations with explicit displayed derivations.
bad_examples: Several paragraphs of computation with no statement of what is being established.
good_examples: To conclude the theorem, it suffices to show that the error term tends to zero.
rules.fix_template: Add a one-sentence roadmap at transition points describing the subgoal and how it connects to the main claim.

rules.title: Use complete English sentences around math
rules.rationale: Sentence fragments around formulas introduce ambiguity and grammatical breaks.
rules.check: Flag fragments like “Since …” followed by a display with no main verb, or displays introduced by incorrect punctuation (e.g., random colon).
rules.pass_criteria: Each paragraph reads as grammatical English when formulas are read aloud.
exceptions: Bulleted lists explicitly labeled as fragments.
Caption-style text in figures/tables.
bad_examples:
If C and P are subsets of N^n, let:
  L(C,P)=...
good_examples: Since x>0, we have x^2>0.
If C and P are subsets of N^n, let L(C,P) denote ...
rules.fix_template: Rewrite fragments into full sentences; adjust punctuation so the display is a grammatical clause of the sentence.

rules.title: Capitalize 'Theorem', 'Lemma', etc. when used as labeled nouns
rules.rationale: Consistent capitalization improves professional readability and aligns with many editorial norms.
rules.check: Flag patterns like “theorem 2”or “lemma 5” when they refer to a labeled result.
rules.pass_criteria: Use “Theorem 2”, “Lemma 5”, “Proposition 3.1” when referring to numbered items.
exceptions: Generic usage: “a theorem of algebra” is not capitalized.
good_examples: As shown in Theorem 2, ...
Lemma 5 implies ...
rules.fix_template: Capitalize the noun when it refers to a labeled result (Theorem/Lemma/Proposition/Corollary/Definition).

rules.title: Avoid gratuitous colons before displays
rules.rationale: Colons are often misused before displayed equations and can create ungrammatical sentences.
rules.check: Flag occurrences like “We have:” or “Let:” immediately before a display when the display merely completes the clause.
rules.pass_criteria: Use a colon only when introducing a list or explanation; otherwise treat the display as a continuation without punctuation after forms of 'to be'.
exceptions: Legitimate list introductions: “as follows:” followed by a list of items.
Algorithm blocks introduced by “Algorithm:” in some venues.
bad_examples:
If C and P are subsets of N^n, let:
  L(C,P)=...
good_examples:
We have
  x = y + 1.

If C and P are subsets of N^n, let L(C,P) be defined by
  L(C,P)=...
rules.fix_template: Remove the colon and rewrite so the displayed equation completes a grammatical sentence.

rules.title: Minimize symbol-heavy prose when words are clearer
rules.rationale: Symbol-dense prose is cognitively expensive and increases scope ambiguity.
rules.check: Flag passages with many quantifier/logic symbols in running text outside displayed formulas.
rules.pass_criteria: Use words for quantifiers and logical connectors in prose; reserve dense notation for displays.
exceptions: Formal logic, proof theory, or papers where symbolic logic is the object of study.
good_examples: For every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that if |x-a|<δ then |f(x)-f(a)|<ε.
rules.fix_template: Move the dense symbolic statement into a display or rewrite it in words in running text.
